category.name: VBA Macros
category.preferred_workflow: Use simple documented VBA project workflows. Load or create macro-enabled documents, access or create VbaProject, modify modules if required, then save the document. Keep validation narrow and task-specific.

core_rules: Create local sample macro-enabled documents when a task refers to an existing file or template.
Do not assume external files already exist.
Always save VBA content in a macro-enabled format.
vba_rules: Use Document.VbaProject to access the VBA project.
Create a new VbaProject when needed before adding modules.
Guard against null or empty module source code before performing text operations.
Prefer clone or template-preserving workflows for reference-sensitive VBA tasks when direct reference construction APIs are not available.
api_safety_rules: Do not invent unsupported VBA APIs.
Do not assume unsupported VbaModule constructors.
Use documented VbaProject, VbaModule, and related APIs only.

common_failure_patterns.symptom: Generated code references members that do not exist in the selected package version.
common_failure_patterns.fix: Replace invented members with documented Aspose.Words APIs already proven in this category.

common_failure_patterns.symptom: The example assumes source files, folders, images, or data already exist.
common_failure_patterns.fix: Create deterministic local inputs before loading, processing, or validating them.

common_failure_patterns.symptom: The example fails at runtime while checking unrelated document internals.
common_failure_patterns.fix: Validate only the requested behavior and the existence of expected outputs.

examples.task: Load a DOCX file containing VBA macros and enumerate all modules in the VbaProject.
Validate that a loaded document's VbaProject contains at least one module before attempting modifications.
Enumerate VbaProject.References, filter out COM references, and log remaining references to console.
Retrieve the source code of a specific VbaModule and write it to a text file for analysis.
Iterate through all VbaModules in a document and replace deprecated API calls with updated equivalents.
Perform a case-insensitive search across all VBA modules for deprecated function names and replace them.
Replace hard-coded file paths in macro source code with relative paths using string manipulation functions.
Update the SourceCode of a VbaModule to include error handling using On Error Resume Next statements.
Iterate through all VbaModules, prepend a comment header containing author and date information to each source code.
Add error logging code to each VBA module by inserting a standardized logging routine at the beginning.
Create a new VbaProject, add a standard module, and assign custom macro code to its SourceCode property.
Create a VBA project with a class module, define a public method, and invoke it from another module.
Add a reference to the Microsoft Excel Object Library in a VbaProject and verify its presence.
Add a reference to the Microsoft Scripting Runtime library and use its Dictionary object within a macro.
Add a reference to a custom COM library, then call its methods from a newly added VBA module.
Remove an existing reference from a VbaProject's References collection and confirm the reference count decreases.
Remove all standard modules from a VbaProject, leaving only class modules, and save the document.
Clone the entire VBA project from one Word document to another and save the target document.
Use the CloneVbaProject method to duplicate a VBA project while preserving module order and references.
Clone a VBA project from a template document into newly generated reports to ensure consistent macros.
Clone a VBA project, then remove all references to external libraries to create a self-contained macro set.
Copy a single VbaModule from a source document's VBA project into a destination document's project.
Load a DOCX file, create a new VBA project, and copy selected modules from another document into it.
Use a configuration file to specify which VBA modules to copy between documents during batch processing.
Import a VBA module from an external .bas file into a document's VbaProject and set its name.
Import macro definitions from a JSON file, create corresponding VbaModules, and assign their source code.
Export all VBA modules from a document to a ZIP archive, maintaining original module filenames.
Export macro source code to JSON format, including module names and code strings, for external processing.
Load multiple DOCM files in a batch, extract each macro's source code, and store them in separate files.
Batch process a folder of DOCM files, extracting macro names and writing them to a CSV summary file.
Compare the source code of two VbaModules from different documents and generate a diff report.
Generate a documentation report listing each VBA module, its type, and the number of lines of code.
Load a DOCX file, add a new VBA module that automates table formatting, and save the updated document.
Use the Document.VbaProject property to replace an existing VBA project with a pre-configured project template.
